text: The surgeon begins the laparoscopic ileocolic resection by carefully detaching the omentum from the ileocolic area.

The surgeon uses a three-port laparoscopic setup with a left paraumbilical 10mm trocar for the camera, a right lower quadrant 5mm port, and a suprapubic 12mm port. The camera visualizes the diseased ileocecal valve and an associated fistula. The surgeon runs the small bowel, confirming no other visible disease except at the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon encounters a fistula tethered to the inferior surface of the ileocecal valve during the laparoscopic colectomy. To improve exposure, lateral mobilization is performed, allowing the surgeon to lift the fistulized area higher off the right iliac fossa for better access.

The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions to isolate the fistula, using a stapler introduced through the suprapubic port to divide the fistula. The stapler is positioned underneath the tissue, closed, and fired, successfully separating the innocent bystander loop from the inflammatory phlegmon in the right iliac fossa. The staple line is inspected, confirming no impingement on the bowel lumen.

The surgeon identifies another loop of bowel stuck inferior to the fistulized loop and performs cranial mobilization to lift the distal root of the small bowel mesentery off the retroperitoneum. The dissection is performed cautiously, avoiding the ureter, with attempts to visualize its proximity. The surgeon approaches the adhered area from an inferior angle, then switches to a superior lateral approach, dissecting from the right colon down towards the area of adherence in the right iliac fossa.
The surgeon dissects through an unviolated plane, providing a clearer view from above. This vantage point aids in identifying the ureter located above the diseased area.

The surgeon lifts a separate loop of small bowel off the retroperitoneum, marking an area with a clip for later inspection. The surgeon bluntly dissects through chronic phlegmon to free the valve from the iliac fossa, then approaches medially, entering the retroperitoneal plane beneath the scarred ileocolic vessels. A window is created under the mesentery, and the ileocolic vessels are taken, preserving the right colic artery.

The surgeon views the adherence from an inferior angle, attempting to elevate it off the retroperitoneum. The dissection reveals a window from the medial approach, allowing the surgeon to separate the tissue at a point where the ureter is definitively absent.

The surgeon creates a window at the planned proximal margin of the thickened small bowel mesentery. The two windows in the mesentery are joined in the thickest area using a vessel sealer. The surgeon prepares for potential bleeding by having sutures and a stapler ready. The ligature successfully seals the vessels, preventing bleeding. The surgeon then lines up for an intracorporeal anastomosis, using a stapler across the planned colon margin while ICG is administered, and staples the small bowel through the suprapubic trocar.

The surgeon positions the camera suprapubically and prepares to staple through the left parumbilical port. The specimen is placed in the pelvis, and the surgeon lines up the isoparastaltic intracorporeal anastomosis using vascular load staplers to minimize bleeding. The first inner row of the suture line is sewn with a 3-0 V-Loc suture from the patient's toes towards the head, and the suture line is then invocated back down.

The surgeon completes the suture line beyond the starting point and places a clip for additional security. The anastomosis is covered with healthy omentum. The suprapubic port site is upsized to a fan steel incision for specimen extraction.
